w:r.w:t: Inspired by chess.com · Designed for TzDraft · Built for Tanzania
chess.com Play with Friends — adapted and improved for TZD
Invite system · Game config · Guest access · Spectators · Rematches · Social layer
1. The Big Picture — Why This Feature Matters

Play with Friends is not a minor feature. It is the single highest-retention mechanic in any competitive game platform. Chess.com knows this — their 'Challenge a Friend' flow is one of the top three entry points for new users. People do not discover draughts alone; they discover it because a friend sent them a link.
Every game invite is a referral. Friend receives a link, creates account, plays. Zero ad spend.
Each TZD game link sent by a Tanzanian player reaches someone new to the platform. First exposure to TZD rules is in the context of a real game with a friend.
Players who play with friends churn 3–5x less than solo players. The social bond becomes the reason to return.
TZD community is tight-knit nationally. Friend games become a reason to check TzDraft daily.
Chess.com allows guests to accept challenges. Many guests later register. Friend games are the #1 guest-to-registered conversion path.
Guests can play without registering. Show them the platform. Convert them after the game.
In Tanzania, draughts (Bao/Draughts culture) is social and communal. Playing with a known friend matches how the game is actually played in real life.

The invite link is the product. Everything else — settings, clocks, rematch — exists to make the game that starts from that link as good as possible. Never add friction between 'I want to play with my friend' and 'we are playing.'
2. What Chess.com Does — And Where We Can Beat Them
Generate a link, share via WhatsApp/SMS/anywhere. No account needed to accept.
Time control, color preference, variant — set before sending.
After game ends, both players see a 'Rematch' button. One click, reversed colors, new game.
After game, share a link to the finished game for others to view.
2.2 Where Chess.com Falls Short (TzDraft Can Win)
Link expires after one use — cannot share to a group
Persistent room links that can be used multiple times. Share once to a WhatsApp group, any member joins.
No lobby/waiting room feel — just a board
A proper waiting room with live countdown, player avatars, game settings visible. Feels like a real match setup.
No spectator support for friend games
Anyone with the link can watch. Show live viewer count. Spectators see the game in real-time with a small delay.
Live text chat panel alongside the board. Not just post-game — during the game too. Critical for friends playing remotely.
'Play while on a call' — deep link to WhatsApp call while game is open. Not integrated, just smart UX.
Rematch only works if both players are still on the page
Rematch sends a notification if the other player navigated away. Reconnects them even after closing the tab.
Friend Games history — see all games played between you and a specific friend. Win/loss record. Analysis of past games.
Remember last used settings per user. Default to their preferred time control.
3. The Complete User Flow — Every Path
3.1 Primary Flow: Registered User Invites a Friend
This is the main flow. Every other flow branches from this one.
— User clicks 'Play with Friend' from navbar, dashboard, or post-game screen
— Configure the game before sending the invite
— Unique invite URL created. Share options shown.
— Host waits. Sees their own avatar + empty opponent slot. Countdown or 'Waiting...'
— Opens URL in any browser. Sees game invite preview.
— Logged in: joins immediately. Guest: plays as guest. New user: quick register.
— Both players visible. Settings confirmed. 3-2-1 countdown begins.
— Board appears. Clock running. Full PvP game under TZD rules.
— Result screen. Rematch button. Share game button. Analysis link. Play again options.
A guest receives the link from a friend. They have never used TzDraft. The entire experience must be frictionless:
Guest opens invite link → sees a branded preview page with: game settings, host name/avatar, 'You've been challenged to a game of Tanzania Draughts!'
Two buttons: 'Play as Guest' (one click, enter name) and 'Create Account' (30 seconds, then auto-joins game)
Guest enters a display name. No email, no password, no verification. Instantly in the waiting room.
Game plays normally. Guest has full board functionality, chat, draw/resign.
After game ends — show conversion prompt: 'Save your game history and rating — create a free account.' Pre-fill with the guest name they used.
Guest session tied to a temporary token stored in localStorage. If they create an account within 24 hours, game history transfers.
Zero forms before the game. The only thing a guest types before playing is their display name — one field, one button. Any more friction and they leave.
3.3 Multi-Use Room Link (The WhatsApp Group Feature)
Unlike chess.com's single-use links, TzDraft supports persistent room links that can be used repeatedly — perfect for sharing in a WhatsApp group or family chat:
— anyone who opens it can join as the next available opponent
Room host is always White by default (configurable). First person to click join becomes Black.
If a game is already in progress when a third person opens the link, they automatically become a spectator.
After game ends, the room resets. Next person to open the link starts a new challenge.
Room links expire after 7 days of inactivity or can be manually cancelled by the host.
WhatsApp groups are the primary communication channel. A single persistent link shared once in a family or club group enables ongoing play without anyone needing to generate new links each time.
The rematch experience must be instant and feel natural — like flipping the board and resetting the pieces in a physical game:
Game ends. Result shown with animation.
If one player clicks Rematch, the other sees a 'Rematch offered' notification with Accept/Decline.
On Accept — colors swap automatically (whoever was White is now Black). Same time control. Game starts with a 3-second countdown.
If the second player closed the tab — send a browser notification or in-app notification: '[Friend] wants a rematch. Click to rejoin.'
Rematch link remains valid for 10 minutes. After that, the room expires.

The setup panel is shown to the HOST before the invite link is generated. It must be fast to configure — most people will just click 'Create' with defaults. Advanced options are there for those who want them.
Random feels fair. Most players keep this.
Friend games default Casual. Both must be registered for Rated.
Off for private games. On by default.
Host can disable for tournament-style single games.
For private club games. Friend must know the password to join.

Show a live preview of what the invite link will look like — including the game settings summary — before the user sends it. 'Your friend will see: 10+5 Rapid · Casual · Spectators allowed.' This reduces setup errors and makes users feel confident about what they are sending.
After the link is created, show rich sharing options tailored to Tanzania:
Pre-composed message: '🎯 I challenge you to a game of Tanzania Draughts! Click here to play: [link]'
One-tap copy. Shows a 'Copied!' confirmation for 2 seconds.
Opens native SMS with pre-filled message. Important for feature phones.
Show a QR code for in-person sharing. Perfect for club nights — display on screen, friends scan with phone.
On mobile, trigger native share sheet — covers all other apps.
5. The Waiting Room — A Feature Chess.com Skips

Chess.com takes you straight to a blank board with 'Waiting for opponent...' text. This feels cold and uncertain. TzDraft can do much better — a proper waiting room that builds anticipation and keeps the host engaged while they wait.
Your avatar + username on the left.
'Resend Link' button, copy link, WhatsApp shortcut
Returns host to lobby and invalidates the link
'Link opened 2 times' — tells the host their friend is looking at it
Before the friend joins, they see a dedicated preview page — not just a blank board. This page is the first impression of TzDraft for new users:
'[HostName] has challenged you to Tanzania Draughts!'
Makes it feel personal. 'Playing against: Amina Hassan · Rating: 1340'
'10 minutes + 5 second increment · Casual game · Spectators allowed'
'Accept Challenge' (big green button) and 'Learn How to Play' (secondary link)
Animated TZD board with pieces — gives flavour while they decide
Name shown, Accept goes straight to waiting room
'Enter your name to play as guest' — single input field below the Accept button
5.3 Both Players in Waiting Room — Game About to Start
The moment the second player joins, both players see the same screen:
White piece icon under host, Black piece icon under joiner (or as configured)
3... 2... 1... Go! — builds excitement, prevents instant-start surprise
Both players see what they are about to play
Host can modify within the 3-second window. Restarts countdown.

Chess.com starts the game the moment both players are present — players are sometimes mid-scroll when the clock starts. A 3-second countdown is respectful and creates a ritual feel, like a referee starting a match.
6. In-Game Features — What Friend Games Add
Chess.com has no in-game chat. This is a significant gap — friends playing online still want to trash-talk, react, and communicate. TzDraft has chat as a first-class feature:
Collapsible side panel next to the board. On mobile: bottom drawer.
Click any past move to react to it specifically: '!! brilliant' or '? mistake'
Automatic profanity filter. One-tap report. Mute button hides chat without closing it.
Guests can chat — name shown as '[GuestName] (Guest)'
Chat history saved with the game record. Viewable in post-game analysis.

Support Swahili and English in chat. Auto-detect language and show chat in the sender's language. If host wrote in Swahili and opponent replies in English, both see both. This makes the feature feel locally built, not imported.
Anyone who opens a friend game link while a game is in progress automatically becomes a spectator:
Read-only board. Moves shown in real-time with a 5-second delay (anti-cheating).
Separate spectator chat channel. Players cannot see spectator messages during the game.
Both players see 'X watching' — adds social pressure and excitement.
Show a position evaluation bar to spectators only (never to players). Shows who is winning without telling the players.
Spectators can chat with players once game ends. Spectator view of the game record.
100 per game (WebSocket room limit). Club tournaments can use this for live broadcasts.
Not available in ranked or casual matchmaking. Available in friend games if both players agree in setup:
Host enables take-backs in game setup (off by default).
Button appears during your opponent's turn: 'Request take-back.' Sends a prompt to opponent.
If accepted, last move reverted. If declined, game continues.
3 take-backs per player per game. Prevents abuse.
Time used on the retracted move is restored minus 5 seconds (penalty).
In ranked matchmaking, aborting is strictly controlled. In friend games the rules are more relaxed because both players know each other:
Either player can abort within the first 2 moves with no rating consequence.
After move 3, abort counts as resignation for the aborting player.
If internet drops, the disconnect grace period is extended to 3 minutes for friend games (vs 60 seconds for ranked).
7. Post-Game Experience — Keep Players Coming Back
The result screen is the most emotionally charged moment. Design it to feel satisfying and to immediately offer the next action:
Winner's side: confetti or piece crown animation. Loser's side: pieces fade. Draw: handshake animation.
Captures made by each side. Move count. Time used. Average move time.
Engine identifies the most impactful single move (biggest swing). Highlighted with '!!'
If both players are registered: 'Your record vs [Friend]: 3 wins, 2 losses, 1 draw'
One-tap to see the full game with engine analysis. No depth — just move quality indicators.
Sends rematch offer to opponent. Colors swap. Same time control.
Opens the game in analysis mode. Engine shows best moves. Available to both players and spectators.
Generates a read-only link to the finished game. Anyone can view — no account required.
Downloads the game in PGN-equivalent format (PDN). For archiving and study.
View the opponent's profile — rating history, game stats, recent games.
If not already friends on the platform — add them to your friends list.
Return to game setup panel. Settings pre-filled from this game.
After a guest finishes their first game, show a single well-timed conversion prompt:
'Great game! Create a free TzDraft account to:
Save your game history · Track your rating · Challenge friends anytime · Access game analysis'
Pre-fill name field with the guest display name they used.
If they click 'Maybe Later', show again once more after their second game. Then stop.
Guest token valid for 24 hours — if they register within that window, game history is automatically imported.
8. Friends System — The Full Social Layer
The Friends system should feel like a lightweight social network specifically for TZD. Not trying to be Facebook — just enough social glue to make players feel connected and motivated to return.
Green dot = online now. Orange dot = in a game (shows opponent name). Grey = offline (shows last seen).
One click on a friend → challenge them directly with your last used settings. Skip the setup panel.
Hover over a friend to see your record: W3 / L2 / D1 · 6 games played.
'Amina just won a Blitz game · David completed 5 games today' — makes the platform feel alive.
Online first, then recently active, then alphabetical.
'Add as friend' button appears on result screen.
'Add Friend' button on any player's profile.
Search by username or display name in the Friends section.
Share a personal profile link — anyone who visits can send a friend request.
Any player you've played against appears in your 'Recent Opponents' — easy one-click add.
With permission, match phone contacts who have TzDraft accounts. Show 'People you may know.'
Send a challenge directly to a friend. They see a notification: 'Baraka challenged you — 10+5 Rapid. Accept?'
Direct challenges expire after 30 minutes if not accepted.
'Challenge me any time this week' — post a standing challenge that friends can accept when convenient.
Browser push notifications, in-app badge, email (daily digest only). Never spam.
A dedicated view showing all games played between you and a specific friend — chess.com does not have this and players love it:
Timeline of every game with result, time control, and date.
'Your record: 12 wins, 8 losses, 3 draws against Amina Hassan'
Longest game, most captures, fastest win, biggest upset.
One-click access to any game's analysis.
'Nemesis badge' — if a friend beats you more than 3x in a row, they get a 'your nemesis' label. Click to challenge them to break the streak.
9. Club Feature — Play with Your Team (Phase 2)
Beyond two-player friend games, TzDraft should support clubs — groups of players who play together regularly. This is where chess.com's Club feature drives massive long-term retention:
A named group with a persistent room link, member list, and club leaderboard.
Club admin creates the club. Invites members via link or username.
Club has its own chat channel — persistent, not tied to individual games.
Club leaderboard shows member rankings within the club.
Teacher creates a club, shares the join link with students. All student games against each other are in the club history.
Create a family club. Grandparents, parents, children all in one place. Play from different cities.
Each regional chapter has a club. Club vs Club matches (Phase 3).
Office draughts league. Lunchtime club. Persistent leaderboard on the company Slack.
Club admin creates a round-robin or Swiss tournament within the club.
Automatic pairings. Players get notifications for their next game.
Winner gets a club trophy badge visible on their profile.
10. Technical Architecture — How to Build It
id TEXT PRIMARY KEY, -- 'inv_abc123' (8 char random)
time_control JSONB NOT NULL, -- { base: 600, increment: 5 }
game_id TEXT REFERENCES games(id), -- filled when game starts
link_views INTEGER DEFAULT 0, -- how many times link was opened
status TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT 'pending', -- pending|accepted|blocked
token TEXT PRIMARY KEY, -- stored in browser localStorage
converted_to TEXT REFERENCES users(id), -- set when guest registers
expires_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT NOW() + INTERVAL '24 hours'
move_ref INTEGER, -- if reacting to a move, which move number
CREATE INDEX idx_game_messages_game ON game_messages(game_id, sent_at);
POST /api/invites → create invite, returns { inviteId, link, qrCode }
GET /api/invites/:id → get invite details (public — for preview page)
GET /api/friends → list friends with online status
GET /api/friends/:id/history → games played between two users
POST /api/challenges → challenge a friend { friendId, settings }
joinInviteRoom { inviteId } → join the waiting room
sendMessage { gameId, content, moveRef? } → chat message
inviteRoomState { host, guest?, settings, status } → full waiting room state
opponentJoined { player } → friend joined the waiting room
newMessage { sender, content, sentAt, moveRef? } → chat message
linkViewed { totalViews } → host sees link was opened
/invite/:inviteId → invite preview page (public, no auth required)
/play/friend → game setup panel (auth required OR guest name step)
async execute(hostId: string, settings: InviteSettings): Promise<InviteResult> {
// Generate QR code as data URL for display
`🎯 Nakuchallenge mchezo wa Tanzania Draughts! Bonyeza hapa ucheze: ${link}`
Phase 1 — Core Friend Game (Week 1–2) — SHIP THIS FIRST
/invite/:id → shows host info, game settings, Accept button.
Display name input → guest session token → game starts.
Basic version — both avatars, game settings, countdown.
Simple version — if both on page, colors swap, new game.
✔ Phase 1 alone is a complete, shippable feature. It lets anyone play with a friend via a link. Ship this and gather feedback before building phases 2 and 3.
Challenge a friend without generating a link.
Anyone with link watches in real-time (5s delay).
For club nights and in-person sharing.
Optional, friend games only, 3 per player.
Post-game prompt with game history transfer.
'Challenge me this week' public challenge posts.
Deep link from result screen to game analysis.
Club round-robin tournaments with automatic pairings.
Live tournament broadcasting (spectators for all club games simultaneously).
12. The One Thing That Makes It Great
Everything above is table stakes — chess.com has most of it. The one thing that makes TzDraft's Play with Friends genuinely special is cultural fit.
WhatsApp is the primary communication layer in Tanzania. The share flow is designed around WA — not email, not Twitter. The pre-composed Swahili message feels native.
Families and clubs do not want to generate a new link every time they play. One link for the whole family group. Share once in June, still playing in December.

The in-game chat supports both languages. A player in Dar es Salaam writes Swahili to their cousin in Arusha. The cousin replies in English. Both messages appear correctly. No one feels like they are using a foreign platform.
Physical draughts clubs are common in Tanzania. A QR code shown on a laptop screen lets every club member join the digital platform instantly, in the room, together.
Friend games enforce full TZD Official Rules v2.2 automatically. Players learn the rules by playing with a friend — the platform teaches them without a tutorial.

Chess.com built 'Play with Friends' for a global audience. TzDraft should build it for Tanzania — the people, the language, the communication habits, the social culture. That is the version no one else can copy.
Tanzania Draughts Federation • Play with Friends — Full Feature Design • TzDraft Product & Engineering • 2026
